content.full_text:
REED & DAVIDSON
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

DANA W. REED
CARY DAVIDSON

888 WEST SIXTH STREET, 12TH FLOOR
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017

ORANGE COUNTY OFFICE
3151 AIRWAY AVENUE, SUITE M-1
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626
TELEPHONE (714) 641-688
FACSIMILE (714) 546-1003

DARRYL B. WOLD
OF COUNSEL

TELEPHONE (213) 362-9236
FACSIMILE (213) 623-1692

January 29, 1990

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Division Chief, Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Pritchard:

This letter requests informal assistance under Government Code,
Section 83114(b). I am requesting this advice as a person with a
duty to advise other persons relating to their duties under the
Political Reform Act ("the Act"). (2 California Code of
Regulations, Section 18329(c)(1)).

We often serve as treasurer for ballot measure and candidate
committees. Section 84303 of the Government Code provides that
no expenditure shall be made by an agent or independent
contractor on behalf of any candidate or committee unless it is
reported by that candidate or committee in the same manner as if
it had been made directly by the candidate or committee. The
agent or independent contractor is required by that section
to make known to the candidate or committee all the information
required to be reported.

Section 18431 of title 2 of the California Code of Regulations
contains a listing of expenditures that must be reported under
Government Code, Section 84303. The regulation states that
expenditures that are not of the type included on the list do not
have to be reported. Section d of Regulation 18431 states that
an agent or independent contractor shall make known to the
candidate or committee all the information required for reporting
the expenditure. The section provides that a sub-agent or sub-
vendor may make the report required to his or her principal, who
shall report the expenditure to the committee or candidate. An
agent or independent contractor is given until no later than
three working days prior to the time the campaign statement
reporting the expenditure is required to be filed, except for
late contribution/late independent expenditures, which must be
reported within 24 hours.

In view of the applicable Government Code section and the
interpretive regulation, our general question is which sub-
vendors need to be disclosed when a committee pays a vendor for
services? Specifically, when a committee or candidate hires a

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Page Two

printer, does the committee or candidate have to disclose 1)
where the printer bought ink or paper? 2) that an outside
lithographer was retained and paid? 3) that folding and sorting
of the material was provided by a sub-vendor? 4) that the fee
paid includes costs of mailing and postage? 5) the names of
people hired solely to work on the printing matter in question?
6) standard commissions paid to the campaign manager or director?

Often a committee or candidate will contract with a phone bank.
Are we, as the campaign treasurer, supposed to list as sub-
vendors the names of the persons making the phone calls, if those
persons have been hired solely for the purpose of calling on
behalf of the specific candidate or committee? Do we need to
include as a sub-vendor the relevant telephone company providing
the phone service for the source of the phone equipment if it has
been rented?

Customary practice in qualifying ballot measures today includes
the retention of a signature gathering firm. Such firms
routinely pay individuals on the basis of the number of
signatures collected. What sub-vendors, if any, need be
disclosed in connection with gathering signatures by a firm
established to do just that.

It is not uncommon for large campaigns that utilize direct mail
services to retain a caging service to receive and deposit checks
and to prepare appropriate schedules for campaign reports. When
a candidate or committee engages such a firm, what sub-vendors,
if any, must be disclosed?

Needless to say, while the law requires a vendor to make the
information available to the treasurer for inclusion in a
campaign statement, vendors often fail to provide such
information. As treasurer, we ask for advice regarding what we
are required to do to obtain necessary sub-vendor information.
Generally, we send a letter similar to the attached to vendors
who may have to disclose sub-vendors. If the vendor fails to
respond, have we satisfied our reporting responsibilities? If
that is not sufficient, then what should the treasurer do to
obtain the information required to be disclosed?

If you have any questions concerning this request, please feel
free to contact us.

Sincerely,

Cary/Davidson

CD:lb

Enclosure

October 20, 1989




RE:     Campaign Statement for the Period
        9/24/89 through 10/21/89


Dear Sir or Madam:

As you may know, the Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended,
requires that certain payments made by an agent or independent
contractor of a committee such as
be
reported in the same detail as if made directly by the committee.
Such payments of $100 or more must be itemized, listing the
vendor's name and address, a description of the goods or services
and the amount of the payment.

Enclosed is a Schedule E-1 (Payments Made By an Agent or
Independent Contractor on Behalf of a Committee) which should be
completed and returned to me, if one is needed for the payments
made to you by

An FPPC Regulation, the relevant portion of which is enclosed for
your information, specifies the types of expenditures made by an
agent or independent contractor which must be itemized. If you
have any such expenditures which need to be itemized, please
complete the E-1 and return it to us no later than noon on
Tuesday, October 28, 1989.

If you have any questions concerning the need to itemize any
expenditures, please do not hesitate to contact us.


Sincerely,


Cary Davidson

CD:lb

sections.question: Which sub-vendors need to be disclosed when a committee pays a vendor for services? Specific questions include: 1) whether to disclose where a printer bought ink or paper; 2) whether to disclose outside lithographers retained; 3) whether to disclose sub-vendors for folding and sorting; 4) whether to disclose costs of mailing and postage included in fees; 5) whether to disclose names of people hired solely for the printing matter; 6) whether to disclose standard commissions paid to campaign managers or directors. Additional questions concern disclosure requirements for phone bank employees and telephone companies, signature gathering firm sub-vendors, caging services for direct mail campaigns, and what treasurers must do when vendors fail to provide sub-vendor information.
sections.question_synthetic: The letter requests FPPC guidance on which sub-vendors and sub-contractors must be disclosed when a committee or candidate pays a primary vendor for services such as printing, phone banking, signature gathering, and direct mail operations. The attorney also asks what steps a treasurer must take when vendors fail to provide necessary sub-vendor disclosure information.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney requesting informal assistance under Gov. Code Section 8314(b). No FPPC response is included in this document. The letter contains detailed questions about sub-vendor disclosure requirements but no formal conclusion section as this is the request, not the response. An example demand letter to vendors is attached as an enclosure.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Which sub-vendors need to be disclosed when a committee pays a vendor for services? Specific questions include: 1) whether to disclose where a printer bought ink or paper; 2) whether to disclose outside lithographers retained; 3) whether to disclose sub-vendors for folding and sorting; 4) whether to disclose costs of mailing and postage included in fees; 5) whether to disclose names of people hired solely for the printing matter; 6) whether to disclose standard commissions paid to campaign managers or directors. Additional questions concern disclosure requirements for phone bank employees and telephone companies, signature gathering firm sub-vendors, caging services for direct mail campaigns, and what treasurers must do when vendors fail to provide sub-vendor information.

embedding.summary: A law firm (Reed & Davidson) requests informal advice from the FPPC regarding sub-vendor disclosure requirements under Political Reform Act Section 84303 and related regulations, with specific questions about disclosure obligations for committees hiring printers, phone banks, signature gatherers, and other vendors. The request includes a sample letter the firm sends to vendors requesting sub-vendor information.
